resume:
Director of Clinical Operations | Value-Based Care Transformation Leader

Keyword profile: Population Health · Value-Based Care · Care Coordination · Clinical Quality · HEDIS · STAR Ratings · Utilization Management · Care Gaps · Risk Adjustment · HCC Coding · Patient Throughput · Length of Stay · Readmission Reduction · Lean Healthcare · EHR Optimization · Epic · Interdisciplinary Rounds · SDOH · Patient Experience · HCAHPS

Summary: Outcomes-obsessed clinical operations leader who operationalizes value-based care at scale, driving quadruple-aim impact across population health, quality, cost, and experience.

Experience
Current — Regional Health System, Director of Clinical Operations. Owns care coordination and quality across a multi-clinic ambulatory network. Leads HEDIS and STAR improvement, readmission-reduction programs, and EHR workflow optimization.

Prior — Integrated Delivery Network, Manager of Care Management. Stood up care-coordination pods and SDOH screening.

Earlier — Acute care nursing leadership.

Signature outcomes (headline form):
- Spearheaded value-based care transformation improving quality outcomes
- Drove readmission reduction through care-coordination redesign
- Closed care gaps at scale via population-health stratification
- Elevated patient experience scores through service-excellence initiatives

Credentials: RN, MSN; Lean Six Sigma Green Belt.
jd: We are seeking a Director of Clinical Operations to lead care coordination and quality across a 12-clinic ambulatory network. Reporting to the VP of Population Health, this role owns three board-level metrics: 30-day all-cause readmission rate (currently 14.2%, target under 10%), HEDIS composite percentile, and care-gap closure rate for the attributed Medicare Advantage population. You will lead a redesign of the care-coordination model, partner with physician leadership on interdisciplinary rounds, and own EHR workflow optimization in Epic. You will inherit a stalled readmission program and a recent dip in STAR ratings. We need a leader who can describe, in concrete operational terms, exactly what they changed and the measurable outcome — not a recitation of value-based-care talking points.

history.q: Walk me through your current scope — clinics, team size, the metrics you own.
Your resume says you 'drove readmission reduction.' Pick the single program you led and tell me what it actually was.
What was the baseline readmission rate, and what did it become?
What specifically did you change in the care-coordination workflow? Walk me through the new step-by-step.
Who did what in that new workflow — the care managers, the pharmacists, the PCPs?
How did you know it was your redesign and not a coding or case-mix change that moved the number?
Tell me about the part that didn't work. What did you try that failed?
How did you get physician buy-in for the new rounding model?
Okay — give me one concrete week in that program. What did you personally do, Monday to Friday, to stand it up?

candidate_last_answer: Great question. So at the end of the day, this was all about operationalizing the quadruple aim and meeting patients where they are. My north star was always patient-centricity, so I leaned into a culture of continuous improvement, broke down silos, and drove cross-functional alignment around our shared mission. I'm a firm believer that you transform care by empowering frontline teams and leveraging data to bend the cost curve. We took a holistic, patient-first approach, rallied the troops around our true-north metrics, and really moved the needle on outcomes. It was about doing well by doing good.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: rallied the troops around our true-north metrics, and really moved the needle

expected-yield: a concrete, de-buzzworded account of what the candidate personally did during one specific week to stand up the readmission program — the actual tasks, who they met with, what artifact or process they created on which day — replacing the rehearsed value-based-care slogans with verifiable operational detail
